retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The dental role of hydroxyapatite is significant, as it constitutes 70% to 80% of the weight of dentin and enamel. Enamel, the hardest substance in the human body, is made up of relatively large hydroxyapatite crystals measuring 25 nm thick, 40 to 120 nm wide, and 160 to 1000 nm long. [6] Unlike bone, enamel lacks collagen; instead, amelogenins and enamelins provide a framework for mineralization. Hydroxyapatite is the primary component of enamel, helping to minimize diffuse reflectivity by filling surface pores, which contributes to the semitranslucent appearance of enamel. [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor and characteristics are assessed using the patient’s history, clinical exam, and imaging, most commonly computed tomography (CT). On CT scan, ameloblastomas appear as radiolucent (radiographically dark) lesions of the maxilla or mandible, often with multilocular or “soap-bubble” appearance, bony expansion and tooth root resorption. They may also be associated with unerupted teeth. A definitive diagnosis of ameloblastoma is with a surgical biopsy, which shows characteristic histologic findings. It is essential to differentiate the entity from a primary intraosseous squamous cell carcinoma. The World Health Organization (WHO) classified malignant ameloblastoma into two types [3] :

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is a prevalent chronic disease. If left untreated, caries may progress to tooth destruction and exodontia. Dental caries is a complex multifactorial disease of individual, biological, behavioral, and environmental factors. [1] The clinical sign of dental carious is a lesion of varying severity, ranging from opacity in the enamel to frank cavitation exposing dentin. Accurately diagnosing dental caries promotes effective treatment planning, prevents disease progression, and supports optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Various classification methods for dental carious lesions have been developed, each with advantages and limitations. Newer classification systems, like the International Caries Detection and Assessment System (ICDAS) and the American Dental Association Caries Classification System (ADA CCS), have evolved from the traditional Black classification to include the current understanding of the carious process, maximize healthy tooth surfaces, and prevent extension of the carious lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenic tumors of the jaw are a group of lesions that originate from remnants of epithelium or ectomesenchyme associated with teeth development. It ranges from hamartomas to true tumors. The fourth edition of WHO histological typing of odontogenic tumors classifies them as benign and malignant. Then, they are subdivided according to the cell of origin to epithelial, ectomesenchymal, or mixed. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectodermal Dysplasia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ectodermal dysplasias (EDs) form a diverse group of inherited disorders characterized by a congenital defect in two or more ectodermal structures, one of which involves hair, teeth, nails, or sweat glands. Other organs derived from embryonic ectoderm include mammary glands, CNS, external ear, melanocytes, cornea, conjunctiva, and lacrimal apparatus. Having accepted this definition of ectodermal dysplasia, PINHEIRO and FREIRE-MAIA subclassified the ectodermal dysplasia into groups depending on the presence of hair(1), nail(2), the tooth(3) or sweat gland(4) abnormality.

retrieved_docs.title: Koplik Spots -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Koplik spots are the peculiar spots present on the buccal mucosa and are considered a diagnostic/pathognomic feature of measles/rubeola in the pre-eruptive stage. The term Koplik spot derives its name from Dr. Henry Koplik of New York, who first described them in 1896.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Pathophysiology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise From Both Odontogenic Epithelium and Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme, With or Without Dental Hard Tissue Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Another true neoplasm is ameloblastic fibroma, in which both epithelial and ectomesenchymal components are neoplastic. This differentiates it from ameloblastic fibro odontoma and odontoma, which are considered to be hamartomatous lesions. [23] Ameloblastic fibro-odontoma and ameloblastic fibrodentinoma have been excluded from the 4th edition of WHO classification for odontogenic lesions. Evidence suggests that once hard dental tissue is formed, these two lesions usually progress to odontomas. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Cysts -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Non-odontogenic cysts are usually discovered during a routine examination. These lesions arise from the non-odontogenic (non-tooth forming) tissue. By definition, the cysts are lined by epithelium. Cysts within the oral cavity vary in their clinical appearance, incidence, histology, behavior, and management. The clinical features, etiology, and treatment modalities for the following lesions will be discussed in this activity: nasopalatine duct cyst, nasolabial cyst, palatal cyst of the neonate, oral lymphoepithelial cyst, and epidermoid cyst.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are specific anatomic landmarks unique to each type of tooth which define them as incisors, molars, canines, etc. The anatomy of the tooth itself consists of the root which is hidden in the gums, and the crown, or visible part. The root of the tooth is anchored to the bone to which it is associated and allows for blood flow and nerve supply to the tooth to maintain viability. This system of ligamentous attachment connecting the tooth to the surrounding socket is called the periodontium. The hard tissue covering the crown is the enamel. The root is covered by cementum, a substance that is a hardy mineral but softer than enamel.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: White spot is a popular term to name a non-cavitated or initial caries lesion. However, it refers to the lesion's color and has no bearing on its activity. That is why it may be confused with other types of dental white lesions, such as dental fluorosis or molar incisor hypomineralization (MIH). [2] The principal differences between these pathologies are their etiology and location. As mentioned previously in this article, the etiology of the initial lesions is dental caries. They are commonly located on plaque accumulation areas: the gingival or cervical margins and the pits and fissures of the molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Clinical Significance -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Molar-incisor hypomineralization (MIH) is the hypomineralization of systemic origin of one to four permanent first molars, frequently associated with affected incisors. It is a qualitative defect of the enamel that happens when ameloblasts are impaired in late amelogenesis stages: mineralization or maturation. It presents as white, yellow, or brown demarcated enamel opacities located, mainly on the vestibular face of the teeth (in the middle of the incisal third), with a clear distinction between the affected and sound enamel. [17] Although the etiology of the lesions is not defined, they have been related to birth prematurity, digestive system disease, asthma, frequent high fever, renal failure, and dioxins.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblasts are of ectodermal origin and derived from oral epithelium. The cells are only present during tooth development that deposit tooth enamel, which forms the outer surface of the crown. Ameloblasts become functional only after odontoblasts form the primary layer of dentin (the layer beneath enamel). The cells eventually become part of the enamel epithelium and eventually undergo apoptosis (cell death) before or after tooth eruption.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reconstructing damaged hard tissue is essential for various reasons, including traumatic or nontraumatic events, congenital abnormalities, and disease. Such damage can significantly impact orthopedic, dental, and maxillofacial surgeries. Research on numerous biomaterials shows that calcium phosphates have been utilized in hard tissue reconstruction for over 6 decades, with hydroxyapatite being the primary material used in orthopedics and dentistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurocutaneous Syndromes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: TSC is also a disorder in which the diagnosis is clinical. For the clinician to make the diagnosis, the patient has to meet two major criteria or 1 major and 2 or more minor criteria. The major criteria are three or more hypopigmented macules of at least five mm of diameter, three or more angiofibromas or a fibrous cephalic plaque, two or more ungual fibromas, a shagreen patch, multiple retinal hamartomas, cortical dysplasias, subependymal nodules, a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma, a cardiac rhabdomyosarcoma, lymphangioleiomyosarcomatosis and two or more angiomyolipomas. Minor criteria are: several small hypopigmented lesions grouped together, also called “confetti” skin lesions, three or more dental enamel pits, two or more intraoral fibromas, a retinal achromic patch, multiple renal cysts, and nonrenal hamartomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- Mount-Hume Classification System
retrieved_docs.content: More than 100 years later, Mount and Hume published a caries classification system following the current and more conservative approach to caries. [3] The Mount-Hume classification system incorporated the advent of fluoride and adhesive restorative materials, the use of which modified the old principles of cavity design. [3] Mount and Hume classified caries based on their site and size. [2] (see Table 2. Mount-Hume Site Classification of Caries. and Table 3. Mount-Hume Size Classification of Caries.) The size classification was updated in 2006 to include Size 0, referring to non-cavitated lesions, and modify the definition of Size 1. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a secondary malignancy and is characterized by the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term ال سرطان النقيلي, which denotes metastatic cancer. This relationship highlights that involvement of head and neck lymph nodes by metastatic carcinoma represents a spread of a primary tumor to a distant site, fitting the definition of secondary malignancy and reflecting the specific histopathologic features associated with metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: The metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, and the metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of occipital bone. Both of these specific bone metastases are encompassed by the broader entity cancer of bone and connective tissue, which has a broader relationship with the Arabic term ورم العظام (bone tumor). Within this broader category, cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to the malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, the malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and also to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). The Arabic phrase سرطان العظم (bone cancer) is classified as ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, linking bone cancer to the malignant upper‑jaw tumor concept. Thus, metastatic bone cancers of the ethmoid and occipital bones are specific manifestations of the general disease entity cancer of bone and connective tissue, which includes various bone‑related neoplasms described in both English and Arabic terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is linked to the broader Arabic concept “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is specifically mapped to “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). In addition, the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) is classified as a malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships indicate that cancer of bone and connective tissue is understood as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor, connecting the general notion of a bone tumor with a precise anatomical manifestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: with “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي”, indicating that bone cancer is considered a type of malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships illustrate how metastatic bone malignancies are categorized within a clinical classification system, how they correspond to specific anatomical sites, and how they are related to broader concepts of bone and connective‑tissue cancer in both English and Arabic medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of frontal bone. The broader category cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to malignant neoplasm of frontal bone and also corresponds to the Arabic term ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). Additionally, the Arabic term سرطان العظم (bone cancer) classifies ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that bone cancer includes malignant tumors of the upper jaw. These interrelated concepts show that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the frontal bone is a specific manifestation within the spectrum of bone and connective tissue cancers, and that both English and Arabic terminology align these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2510
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant, is linked to the Arabic term “النقيلة,” indicating that this word is used to refer to a lesion whose benign or malignant nature has not yet been determined.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 6 -- Community 2932
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is recognized as an etiological descriptor that can be applied to several clinical entities. It may be used to indicate the cause of ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth), تمزق (rupture or tear), and حَصاة (stone formation). Recognizing these etiological links helps clinicians accurately document and address the underlying causes of these conditions.
